column1: Name the three basic chemical components of a DNA nucleotide.
column2: A pentose sugar (deoxyribose), a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base.

column1: In terms of ring structure, how do purines differ from pyrimidines?
column2: Purines consist of double carbon–nitrogen rings, while pyrimidines consist of a single ring.

column1: Which two nitrogenous bases found in DNA are classified as purines?
Which two nitrogenous bases found in DNA are classified as pyrimidines?
What type of strong covalent bond links the sugar-phosphate backbone of a DNA strand?
How are the two strands of the DNA double helix held together between the nitrogenous bases?
According to the rules of complementary base pairing, Adenine always pairs with _____.
According to the rules of complementary base pairing, Guanine always pairs with _____.
Why is a $G-C$ base pair more stable than an $A-T$ base pair?
column2: $G-C$ pairs form three hydrogen bonds, whereas $A-T$ pairs form only two.

column1: Define the 'antiparallel' orientation of the two DNA strands in a double helix.
column2: The strands run in opposite directions with opposite chemical polarities ($5'$ to $3'$ vs $3'$ to $5'$).

column1: The _____ end of a DNA strand carries a free phosphate group attached to the sugar ring.
The _____ end of a DNA strand carries an unlinked hydroxyl ($-OH$) group attached to the sugar ring.
How many base pairs are typically found in one full turn of the DNA double helix?
What is the physical distance between adjacent nucleotide pairs along the axis of the DNA helix?
Name the two grooves of differing widths created by the coiling of the DNA strands.
column2: The major groove and the minor groove.

column1: At the first level of DNA packaging, the molecule is wound around a histone protein core to form a _____.
Why is DNA replication described as 'semiconservative'?
column2: Each daughter DNA molecule consists of one original parent strand and one newly synthesised strand.

column1: What is the function of 'replication origins' in a DNA molecule?
column2: They are specific sequences where initiator proteins bind to begin prying the DNA strands apart.

column1: Describe the role of initiator proteins during the start of DNA replication.
column2: They bind to the origin and break the hydrogen bonds between bases to unzip a short region of the helix.

column1: What name is given to the Y-shaped junctions where the replication machine moves along the DNA?
In which chemical direction does DNA polymerase always synthesise a new DNA strand?
How does DNA polymerase determine which nucleotide to add to the growing strand?
column2: By monitoring the complementary base pairing between the incoming nucleoside triphosphate and the template strand.

column1: What provides the energy required for the polymerisation of a new DNA strand?
column2: The hydrolysis of high-energy phosphate bonds in the incoming deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates.

column1: Distinguish between the leading strand and the lagging strand at a replication fork.
column2: The leading strand is synthesised continuously, while the lagging strand is synthesised discontinuously in fragments.

column1: What are Okazaki fragments?
column2: Short lengths of DNA synthesised discontinuously on the lagging strand.

column1: Why does DNA polymerase require an RNA primer to begin synthesis?
column2: DNA polymerase can only add nucleotides to an existing $3'$ end; it cannot start a new strand from scratch.

column1: Which enzyme is responsible for synthesising short RNA primers on the lagging strand?
What is the primary function of DNA helicase at the replication fork?
column2: It uses the energy of ATP hydrolysis to unwind the DNA double helix ahead of the replication machine.

column1: Describe the function of single-strand DNA-binding (SSB) proteins during replication.
column2: They cling to exposed single strands to prevent them from re-forming base pairs or being degraded.

column1: What is the purpose of the 'sliding clamp' protein in the replication machine?
column2: It keeps the DNA polymerase firmly attached to the template strand while allowing it to move forward.

column1: Which enzyme uses ATP hydrolysis to lock the sliding clamp onto the DNA molecule?
How are RNA primers removed from the lagging strand once DNA synthesis is complete?
column2: They are degraded by nucleases (such as RNase H).

column1: Which enzyme joins Okazaki fragments together by sealing the nicks in the sugar-phosphate backbone?
Define 'proofreading' as performed by DNA polymerase.
column2: The enzyme checks if the previously added nucleotide is correctly base-paired before adding the next one.

column1: In what direction does the exonuclease activity of DNA polymerase operate during proofreading?
What is the role of the enzyme 'telomerase' in eukaryotic cells?
column2: It replicates the ends of linear chromosomes (telomeres) to prevent them from shortening during each division cycle.

column1: Why is DNA replication in bacterial and eukaryotic chromosomes described as 'bidirectional'?
column2: Two replication forks form at each origin and move away from each other in opposite directions.

column1: How many replication forks are typically active at a single replication origin?
What is the approximate speed of replication fork movement in humans?
DNA replication occurs during which phase of the eukaryotic cell cycle?
The chemical linkage between the $3'$ hydroxyl group of one sugar and the $5'$ phosphate of the next is called a _____ bond.
What is the total width of the DNA double helix?
Which carbon atom of the deoxyribose sugar is the nitrogenous base attached to?
True or False: The lagging-strand DNA polymerase must be released and reattached for every Okazaki fragment.
What byproduct is released and then further hydrolysed when a dNTP is added to a DNA strand?
Why do replication origins typically have sequences rich in $A-T$ base pairs?
column2: $A-T$ pairs have fewer hydrogen bonds, making them easier for initiator proteins to pull apart.

column1: What happens to the RNA primer once it has been removed by nucleases?
column2: It is replaced with DNA by a repair DNA polymerase.

column1: In a human cell, approximately how many 'letters' (nucleotides) are copied during one round of division?
column2: 6 billion (in a diploid cell; 3 billion per haploid set).

column1: The 'backstitching' mechanism of DNA synthesis refers specifically to the replication of the _____ strand.
What term describes the large multienzyme complex that carries out DNA replication?
The repetitive DNA sequence added by telomerase provides a template for _____ to complete the lagging strand.
What is the primary difference between the sugar in DNA and the sugar in RNA?
column2: DNA contains deoxyribose (lacks an $-OH$ group at the $2'$ position), while RNA contains ribose.
